source_paper_title: Risk-Averse Markov Decision Processes Through a Distributional Lens
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Cheng_2025, title={Risk-Averse Markov Decision Processes Through a Distributional Lens}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0211}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0211}, number={3}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Cheng, Ziteng and Jaimungal, Sebastian}, year={2025}, month=aug, pages={1707–1733} }
context:
Page 14, Remark 2.12 (problem formulation discussion)

Another potentially promising approach to address uncertainty in the discount factor \(\gamma\) involves considering the limit of \(\varsigma^{X,\gamma}_{0,\infty}(C(X,A))\) as \(\gamma \to 1\). In the risk-neutral setting with discrete state and action spaces, it has been shown that this limit while scaled properly coincides with average optimality (cf. [51, Corollary 8.2.5], [30]). However, the equivalence of this limit in current context remains unclear.
title: Equivalence between discount-to-one limit and average optimality in risk-averse MDPs
background_latex:
Consider a discrete-time controlled Markov process with state space \(X\) and action space \(A\), with (possibly randomized) Markov policies \(p=(\pi_t)_{t\in\mathbb{N}}\), where each \(\pi_t(x)\) is a probability measure on \(A\) supported on an admissible set \(A_t(x)\subseteq A\). Let \(P(t,x,a,\cdot)\) be a transition kernel on \(X\), and let \(C_t(x,a,x')\in\mathbb{R}\) be a bounded one-step cost. 

Fix a discount factor \(\gamma\in(0,1)\). A (time- and state-dependent) law-invariant convex risk measure at the distributional level is a functional \(\sigma_{t,x}\) mapping the law \(\mu\) of a bounded real random variable to a value in \(( -\infty,\infty]\), satisfying (i) translation invariance \(\sigma_{t,x}(\mu*\delta_c)=\sigma_{t,x}(\mu)+c\), (ii) monotonicity with respect to first-order stochastic dominance, and (iii) convexity with respect to mixtures (as formalized on probability measures). A dynamic risk measure (DRM) \(\varsigma^{X,\gamma}_{0,\infty}(C)\) is constructed by nesting/composing the one-step risk mappings \(\sigma_{t,X_t}\) applied to regular conditional distributions of future (discounted) cost streams under a controlled process \((X,A)\).

For each \(\gamma\in(0,1)\), define the infinite-horizon discounted risk-averse performance criterion (value) under an admissible policy (or controlled process) as \(\varsigma^{X,\gamma}_{0,\infty}(C)\). In classical risk-neutral MDPs with finite state/action spaces, the scaled limit \((1-\gamma)\, \mathbb{E}[\sum_{t\ge 0} \gamma^t C_t]\) as \(\gamma\uparrow 1\) coincides with the optimal long-run average cost under suitable conditions; the paper highlights that an analogous statement for the present risk-averse DRM framework is not established.
problem_definition_latex:
Establish conditions under which the discount-to-one limit of the discounted infinite-horizon distributional dynamic risk measure recovers an average-optimality criterion. Concretely, analyze whether there is an average-cost (or average-risk) optimality notion \(\mathrm{Avg}(C)\) for the risk-averse MDP such that, for appropriate scaling and under suitable assumptions,
\[
\lim_{\gamma\uparrow 1}\, (1-\gamma)\, \inf_{(X,A)\in\Psi}\, \varsigma^{X,\gamma}_{0,\infty}(C) 
\quad\text{equals}\quad
\inf_{\text{admissible policies}}\, \mathrm{Avg}(C),
\]
or otherwise characterize the limit \(\lim_{\gamma\uparrow 1} \varsigma^{X,\gamma}_{0,\infty}(C)\) (possibly after scaling) and its relationship to an average optimality objective in this risk-averse setting.
latex:
Consider a discrete-time controlled Markov process with state space \(X\) and action space \(A\), with (possibly randomized) Markov policies \(p=(\pi_t)_{t\in\mathbb{N}}\), where each \(\pi_t(x)\) is a probability measure on \(A\) supported on an admissible set \(A_t(x)\subseteq A\). Let \(P(t,x,a,\cdot)\) be a transition kernel on \(X\), and let \(C_t(x,a,x')\in\mathbb{R}\) be a bounded one-step cost.

Establish conditions under which the discount-to-one limit of the discounted infinite-horizon distributional dynamic risk measure recovers an average-optimality criterion. Concretely, analyze whether there is an average-cost (or average-risk) optimality notion \(\mathrm{Avg}(C)\) for the risk-averse MDP such that, for appropriate scaling and under suitable assumptions,
\[
\lim_{\gamma\uparrow 1}\, (1-\gamma)\, \inf_{(X,A)\in\Psi}\, \varsigma^{X,\gamma}_{0,\infty}(C) 
\quad\text{equals}\quad
\inf_{\text{admissible policies}}\, \mathrm{Avg}(C),
\]
or otherwise characterize the limit \(\lim_{\gamma\uparrow 1} \varsigma^{X,\gamma}_{0,\infty}(C)\) (possibly after scaling) and its relationship to an average optimality objective in this risk-averse setting.
open_problem_quote: Another potentially promising approach to address uncertainty in the discount factor \(\gamma\) involves considering the limit of \(\varsigma^{X,\gamma}_{0,\infty}(C(X,A))\) as \(\gamma \to 1\). In the risk-neutral setting with discrete state and action spaces, it has been shown that this limit while scaled properly coincides with average optimality (cf. [51, Corollary 8.2.5], [30]). However, the equivalence of this limit in current context remains unclear.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can search over small finite-state/action MDPs and specific law-invariant convex risk measures (e.g., entropic or CVaR-based nested risk) to numerically test whether a proposed scaling limit matches a candidate average-cost notion. However, the space of admissible DRMs and technical conditions is broad, and failures may depend on subtle regularity assumptions, making brute-force falsification only moderately feasible.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary code loop could parameterize finite MDP instances and families of state-dependent risk measures, then optimize policies and estimate \(\varsigma^{X,\gamma}_{0,\infty}\) for \(\gamma\) near 1 to detect convergence patterns and hypothesize correct scaling/average criteria. The fitness signal (numerical discrepancy between scaled discounted values and candidate average-risk values) is computable, but expensive and potentially noisy due to near-unit discounting and nested risk evaluation.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open problem, highlighted in Remark 2.12 of Cheng–Jaimungal, asks for an analogue of the classical Abelian limit linking discounted and average criteria when the performance functional is a time-consistent distributional dynamic risk measure built by nesting state-dependent law-invariant convex one-step risk mappings \(\sigma_{t,x}\). Existing risk-averse MDP theory (notably Ruszczyński’s risk transition mapping framework and subsequent extensions) provides discounted dynamic programming principles and existence of optimal Markov policies under compactness/continuity assumptions, but does not establish what happens as \(\gamma\uparrow 1\) nor whether a scaled limit \((1-\gamma)\inf \varsigma^{X,\gamma}_{0,\infty}(C)\) matches an average-risk optimal value.

Partial progress exists in the direction of defining and analyzing average-type risk criteria for nested risk measures. In particular, Shen–Stannat–Obermayer study risk-sensitive Markov control processes and include an average dynamic risk measure formulation with corresponding optimality equations under strong regularity assumptions, indicating that an average-risk Bellman/Poisson equation is plausible in the nested-risk setting. Work on recursive/iterated coherent risk measures (e.g., Chu–Zhang; Bäuerle–Glauner) further develops infinite-horizon dynamic programming and, in some cases, long-run criteria, but stops short of proving a discount-to-one limit theorem, especially in the distributional DRM setting with state-dependent \(\sigma_{t,x}\).

A promising direction is to develop Abelian/Tauberian theorems for the nonlinear (generally non-affine) Bellman operators \(S_t\) induced by \(\sigma_{t,x}\), potentially via ergodicity/unichain assumptions and a nonlinear Perron–Frobenius or additive eigenvalue approach (risk-sensitive control suggests such structures). Risk-neutral modern treatments of Blackwell/average optimality reductions (e.g., Grand-Clément–Petrik) may provide templates, but new arguments are needed because the nested risk criterion breaks linearity and may require additional properties (e.g., positive homogeneity/coherency, or specific Kusuoka/spectral forms) to obtain a well-defined average-risk constant and a bias function solving a risk-averse Poisson equation.

sources.title: Risk-averse dynamic programming for Markov decision processes
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces risk transition mappings and a time-consistent nested risk criterion for discounted MDPs; establishes finite-horizon and infinite-horizon discounted dynamic programming equations and existence of optimal Markov policies under suitable continuity/compactness assumptions.
sources.connection:
Provides the foundational discounted risk-averse DPP framework that Cheng–Jaimungal generalize distributionally; however it does not establish a 
\(\gamma\uparrow 1\) limit theorem linking discounted risk value to an average-risk criterion.

sources.title: Risk-sensitive Markov control processes
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies risk-sensitive control with dynamic risk measures, including an average dynamic risk measure formulation, and derives corresponding optimality equations under strong regularity (e.g., strong Feller-type) assumptions.
sources.connection: Directly relevant because it treats an average-risk (non-discounted) counterpart for nested risk criteria; suggests what an average optimality equation might look like, but does not prove equivalence with the scaled discounted criterion as \(\gamma\uparrow 1\) in the distributional DRM setting.

sources.title: Markov decision processes with iterated coherent risk measures
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops infinite-horizon dynamic programming for MDPs under iterated coherent (time-consistent) risk measures, allowing randomized policies and unbounded costs under additional assumptions.
sources.connection: Provides another discounted infinite-horizon risk-averse DPP baseline; does not address the \(\gamma\uparrow 1\) limit or average optimality equivalence, but its operator-theoretic setup is a natural starting point for Abelian/Tauberian analyses.

sources.title: Markov decision processes with recursive risk measures
sources.theorem_or_result: Establishes discounted and (under additional structure) long-run criteria for MDPs with recursive (nested) risk measures, including existence of optimal policies and dynamic programming under unbounded costs.
sources.connection: Represents progress toward understanding non-discounted/long-run formulations for recursive risk; useful for formulating an average-risk objective compatible with time consistency, but does not settle the discount-to-one limit equivalence in the distributional DRM framework.

sources.title: Erratum to: Risk-averse dynamic programming for Markov decision processes
sources.theorem_or_result: Clarifies and corrects assumptions/results in the 2010 risk-averse dynamic programming framework, including technical conditions for the infinite-horizon discounted case.
sources.connection: Ensures the discounted theory is on solid footing before attempting \(\gamma\uparrow 1\) asymptotics; does not address average optimality equivalence.

sources.title: Distribution-invariant risk measures, information, and dynamic consistency
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops distribution-invariant (law-invariant) dynamic risk measures and analyzes dynamic consistency from an information/distributional perspective.
sources.connection: Conceptual precursor to Cheng–Jaimungal’s distributional DRM construction; informs what kinds of time-consistent law-invariant DRMs might admit long-run limits, but does not treat MDP average optimality or discount-to-one limits.

sources.title: Reducing Blackwell and average optimality to discounted MDPs via the Blackwell discount factor
sources.theorem_or_result: Provides reductions connecting average-cost and Blackwell optimality to discounted MDPs via a Blackwell discount factor in the risk-neutral setting, with algorithmic implications.
sources.connection: While risk-neutral, it supplies modern Abelian/Tauberian-style techniques and structural conditions for discount-to-one/average equivalences that could inspire analogous results for risk-averse nested risk criteria.
